summary: Napoleon Bonaparte was the Corsican-born general who rose through the chaos of the French Revolution to command the Republican artillery at the Siege of Toulon, seize power in the Coup of 18 Brumaire, and crown himself Emperor of the French in 1804. His conquests across Europe -- from Austerlitz to Jena to Friedland -- reshaped the continent through the Napoleonic Code and the Continental System, but his empire unraveled after the disastrous 1812 invasion of Russia, culminating in abdication, exile to Elba, a brief return during the Hundred Days, and final defeat at the Battle of Waterloo. He died in exile on Saint Helena in 1821. He is among the most frequently painted, written-about, and quoted figures in Western history, from Jacques-Louis David's equestrian portrait of him crossing the Alps to his role as the historical backdrop of Tolstoy's War and Peace.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Napoleon is identified in giveaways as the French general and emperor who conquered much of Europe, invaded Russia in 1812, and was ultimately exiled after his defeat at the Battle of Waterloo -- the same figure repeatedly painted by Jacques-Louis David and used as the historical backdrop of Tolstoy's War and Peace.

clues.clue: Conquered most of Europe as Emperor of the French, was exiled to Elba following the 1812 invasion of Russia, briefly returned to power, and was finally defeated at the Battle of Waterloo before being exiled to Saint Helena
examples: name this man who was exiled to Elba following the French invasion of Russia and then ultimately defeated at the Battle of Waterloo.
name this man who conquered most of Europe and was defeated at Waterloo.

clues.clue: Repeatedly painted by Jacques-Louis David -- at his coronation, in his study, and crossing the Alps
examples: name this monarch depicted at his coronation, in his study, and crossing the Alps by Jacques-Louis David.
name this leader whom Jacques-Louis David painted "in his Study" and "crossing the Alps."

clues.clue: His 1812 invasion of Russia is the historical backdrop of Leo Tolstoy's novel War and Peace
examples: name this character, whose 1812 Invasion of Russia is the backdrop of Leo Tolstoy's novel War and Peace.
War and Peace is set during what Frenchman's invasion of Russia?

works.name: Early Life, Corsica & Rise to Power
works.description: Born in Corsica while it was under French control, the young Napoleon led riots against French troops on the island before joining the Republican army, where he commanded the artillery that helped recapture the port of Toulon from Royalist and British forces in 1793, launching his military career.

clues.clue: Born on Corsica while it was under French control, and as a young man led riots against French troops there
examples: This man was born on Corsica while it was controlled by France. Despite leading riots against French troops there, he later became emperor of France and invaded Russia before losing at Waterloo.

clues.clue: As a young artillery officer, commanded the Republican artillery at the Siege of Toulon (1793), beginning his rise to prominence
examples: This Corsican commanded the Republican artillery during the Siege of Toulon, on his path to crowning himself Emperor of the French in 1804.

works.name: The Directory & the Coup of 18 Brumaire
works.description: As a rising general under the Directory, Napoleon dissolved the leftist Pantheon Club, tied to Francois-Noel "Gracchus" Babeuf's Conspiracy of the Equals, before engineering the Coup of 18 Brumaire (1799) with his brother Lucien, President of the Council of Five Hundred, overthrowing the Directory to become First Consul and effectively ending the French Revolution.

clues.clue: Overthrew the Directory in the Coup of 18 Brumaire (1799) and became First Consul, with the assistance of his brother, President of the Council of Five Hundred, who convinced the legislature to relocate to the Chateau de Saint-Cloud
examples: identify this event in which Napoleon overthrew the Directory.
The coup of 18 Brumaire led to the ascension of this man to the First Consulship. In 1804, this man became Emperor of the French.

clues.clue: As a general under the Directory, dissolved the leftist Pantheon Club (linked to Babeuf's Conspiracy of the Equals) before later overthrowing the Directory himself
examples: The Pantheon Club, many of whose members made up the Conspiracy of Equals, was earlier dissolved by this man as a way of preserving the Directory's power. This man later overthrew the Directory on the...

clues.clue: When accused of violating the Constitution during the coup, claimed his opponents had already destroyed it, prompting opponents to shout "Caesar-Cromwell-Tyrant!" at him, after which grenadiers were called in and Paul Barras was bribed to resign
examples: When accused of violating the Constitution, this event's instigator claimed his opponents had already destroyed it, after which opponents shouted "Caesar-Cromwell-Tyrant!" at him.

clues.clue: In the coup's aftermath, Roger Ducos was elevated and the Constitution of the Year VIII was created
examples: In the aftermath of this event, Roger Ducos was elevated in position and the Constitution of Year VIII was created.

works.name: The Italian & Egyptian Campaigns
works.description: Napoleon's 1798 invasion of Egypt, intended to disrupt British interests in South Asia, saw him defeat the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids and capture Malta en route, but Horatio Nelson's victory at the Battle of the Nile stranded his army; he was later checked by Jazzar Pasha's resistance (aided by the blind Jew Haim Farhi and British officer William Sidney Smith) at the Siege of Acre, executed thousands of mostly Albanian prisoners at Jaffa, and issued a proclamation to the Egyptian people defending his devotion to Islam before the campaign ended with his subordinate Jacques-Francois de Menou's surrender in the Capitulation of Alexandria.

clues.clue: Invaded Egypt in 1798 to disrupt British colonial interests in South Asia, defeating the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids and capturing Malta en route
examples: who was this man, who sought to disrupt British colonial interests in South Asia with his 1798 invasion of Egypt?
This vanquisher of the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids lost the Battle of the Nile, forcing him to evacuate his invasion of Egypt.

clues.clue: Horatio Nelson's victory over his fleet at the Battle of the Nile forced him to evacuate his Egyptian invasion
examples: identify this foe of Horatio Nelson who shortly thereafter proclaimed himself Emperor of France in 1804.

clues.clue: Was checked at the Siege of Acre by the resistance of Jazzar Pasha (aided by the blind Jew Haim Farhi), later crediting William Sidney Smith's British artillery shipments with forcing his retreat
examples: One of this figure's pivotal efforts was foiled by the steadfast resistance of the blind Jew Haim Farhi and Jezzer Pasha.
This man credited William Sidney Smith's artillery shipments with forcing him to abandon the aforementioned siege of Acre.

clues.clue: At Jaffa, executed thousands of Jazzar Pasha's mostly-Albanian troops as prisoners of war
examples: At Jaffa, Napoleon executed thousands of Jazzar Pasha's troops, who were mostly of this ethnicity.

clues.clue: Issued a proclamation to the Egyptian people affirming his devotion to Islam (printed on Egypt's first Arabic printing press and dissected in a critical response by the chronicler al-Jabarti), teasing a personal conversion
examples: A letter that this man sent to the Egyptian people was dissected and critiqued in a long response by al-Jabarti.
Egypt's first Arabic printing press may have been used by this leader for a proclamation that began and ended with the shahada, teasing a conversion to Islam.

clues.clue: The Egyptian campaign ended when his subordinate Jacques-Francois de Menou surrendered in the Capitulation of Alexandria
examples: A campaign initiated by this man ended when his subordinate Jacques-Francois de Menou surrendered in the Capitulation of Alexandria.

works.name: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms
works.description: As First Consul and Emperor, Napoleon promulgated the Napoleonic Code, a unified civil code that replaced France's decentralized feudal-era coutumes and purged the opposition Tribunate (including Benjamin Constant); he also restored the pre-Revolutionary French nobility by an 1804 law, reconciled with Pope Pius VII through the 1801 Concordat while retaining the right to nominate bishops, and convened the Grand Sanhedrin to formalize Jewish emancipation in his empire.

clues.clue: Promulgated the Napoleonic Code, a unified civil code replacing the decentralized coutumes of the feudal era; the Tribunate was purged of opponents, including Benjamin Constant, for resisting it
examples: name this basis of modern French civil law, named for the emperor who introduced it.
It was created to replace the decentralized system of coutumes from the feudal era.

clues.clue: Said the Code would be remembered longer than his "40 battles," such as Ulm and Jena
examples: Its namesake said it would be remembered better than his victories in "40 battles," such as Ulm and Jena.

clues.clue: The Code's opening section on "persons" forbade women from entering into contracts or from divorce by mutual consent
examples: This document's first section, named for "persons," forbids women from entering into contracts and divorce by mutual consent.

clues.clue: Convened the Grand Sanhedrin, an assembly of European Jewish communities chaired by Rabbi David Sinzheim, whom he gave the rank of "nasi"
examples: This ruler gave Rabbi Joseph David Sinzheim the rank of "nasi" and established Grand Sanhedrin for Jews in this ruler's realm.

clues.clue: Restored the legal existence of the French nobility (the Second Estate) with an 1804 law, ending the abolition that had lasted since 1789
examples: This group didn't legally exist from 1789 until an 1804 law passed by Napoleon.

clues.clue: Reconciled with Pope Pius VII through the 1801 Concordat, retaining the right to nominate bishops
examples: He retained the right to nominate bishops in a reconciliation with Pope Pius VII and won a pyrrhic victory over Kutuzov at Borodino.

works.name: Coronation as Emperor (1804)
works.description: In December 1804, Napoleon crowned himself Emperor of the French at Notre Dame, breaking with tradition by holding the ceremony outside Rheims; Pope Pius VII presided, and Napoleon hastily married Josephine in a religious ceremony beforehand (arranged by his uncle, Cardinal Joseph Fesch) so she could participate, in an event later immortalized by Jacques-Louis David's painting.

clues.clue: Crowned himself Emperor of the French in a December 1804 ceremony at Notre Dame, presided over by Pope Pius VII, which broke with tradition by not taking place in Rheims
examples: name this event in which a Corsican crowned himself Emperor of France.
This event broke with past traditions by not taking place in Rheims and involved an oath promising to uphold a Concordat signed 3 years earlier.

clues.clue: Was hastily given a religious marriage to Josephine by his uncle, Cardinal Joseph Fesch, so that she could participate in the coronation
examples: The central participant in this event was hastily given a church marriage by his ordained uncle, Joseph Fesch, in order to be able to participate in it without issue.

clues.clue: The coronation was delayed from its planned date, the anniversary of 18 Brumaire
examples: This event had to be delayed from its planned date of the anniversary of 18 Brumaire.

clues.clue: According to a dubious legend, a balloon released to celebrate the coronation crossed the Alps and landed on the tomb of Emperor Nero
examples: A hard to believe story claims that, to celebrate this event, a balloon was released which crossed the Alps and landed ominously on the tomb of Emperor Nero.

works.name: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit
works.description: Napoleon crushed the Third Coalition at the Battle of Austerlitz (1805) after encircling General Karl Mack's Austrians in the bloodless Ulm maneuver, then shattered Prussia in the Jena campaign of 1806 with generals like Louis Davout and Jozef Poniatowski, before defeating Russia and securing the Treaties of Tilsit (1807) with Tsar Alexander I -- signed on a raft on the Neman River -- which created the Confederation of the Rhine and brought Russia into his Continental System.

clues.clue: Won the Battle of Austerlitz (1805), defeating the combined armies of Alexander I of Russia and Francis I of Austria in the War of the Third Coalition
examples: name this French emperor who won the Battle of Austerlitz.
This monarch had earlier defeated an alliance of Alexander I of Russia and Francis I of Austria during the War of the Third Coalition.

clues.clue: Completed the "Ulm maneuver," encircling General Karl Mack's Austrian army and forcing its surrender without a major battle
examples: this man's forces successfully completed the Ulm maneuver.

clues.clue: In the 1806 Jena campaign against Prussia, marched his army through the Franconian Forest to the Saale River, catching a Prussian rearguard commanded by Hohenlohe; his generals included Jozef Poniatowski and Louis Davout
examples: In one military campaign, this man marched his army through the Franconian Forest to the Saale River, catching an enemy rearguard commanded by Hohenlohe.
Generals who served this man included Jozef Poniatowski and Louis Davout.

clues.clue: Signed the Treaties of Tilsit (1807) with Tsar Alexander I of Russia on a raft on the Neman River, creating the Confederation of the Rhine and bringing Russia into the Continental System
examples: This monarch set up the Confederation of the Rhine, which was acknowledged by Alexander I of Russia in the Treaties of Tilsit.
One of these two leaders ceded the Ionian islands to the other, and agreed to his Continental System, as part of a treaty they signed on a raft on the Niemen river at Tilsit.

clues.clue: Sought alliances beyond Europe: signed the never-enacted Treaty of Finckenstein with Fath Ali Shah of Qajar Iran, and sent a letter (intercepted at Muscat) proposing an alliance to an Omani ruler
examples: Though its terms were never enacted, this ruler signed the Treaty of Finckenstein with Fath Ali Shah.
A letter from this man to another ruler claimed that he had a "vast and invincible army... to save you from the iron yoke" of another power, but it was intercepted at Muscat.

works.name: The Continental System & the Peninsular War
works.description: Napoleon's Continental System, an embargo begun by the Berlin and Milan Decrees to shut Britain out of European trade, provoked the British bombardment of Copenhagen and forced the Portuguese royal family to flee to Brazil; his 1807 invasion of Portugal to enforce the blockade opened the Peninsular War in Spain and Portugal, where the Duke of Wellington's guerrilla-aided victories -- including at Salamanca and, decisively, at Vitoria -- eventually drove out his forces despite the defensive Lines of Torres Vedras.

clues.clue: Began the Continental System, an embargo of the United Kingdom ("a nation of shopkeepers") by much of Europe, launched with the Berlin and Milan Decrees; Britain's response included Lord Cathcart's bombardment of Copenhagen
examples: name this system by which Napoleon tried to keep Britain out of Europe's trade.
This policy was begun by the Berlin Decree and strengthened by the Milan Decree.

clues.clue: Non-compliance with the Continental System forced Portugal's House of Braganza (John VI and Maria I) to flee with their court to Brazil, and Russia's 1812 withdrawal from the system prompted his disastrous invasion
examples: The House of Braganza's refusal to comply with this policy led to a war which forced them to flee to Brazil.
In violation of the Treaty of Tilsit, Russia ended cooperation with this policy in 1812, leading the Grande Armee to conduct a disastrous invasion.

clues.clue: Invaded Portugal and Spain, opening the Peninsular War (which coined the term "guerrilla warfare"), after a meeting at Bayonne in which Charles IV and his son abdicated in favor of Napoleon's brother
examples: The term "guerrilla" was coined during this conflict in which the Battle of Salamanca was won by the Duke of Wellington.
At a meeting in Bayonne that provoked this conflict, Charles IV and his son both abdicated in favor of the host ruler's brother.

clues.clue: His marshal Andre Massena was forced to retreat from Portugal after failing to breach the secretly-constructed Lines of Torres Vedras defending Lisbon
examples: Andre Massena was forced to retreat during this conflict due to secret forts constructed along the Lines of Torres Vedras.

clues.clue: The Duke of Wellington's decisive victory at the Battle of Vitoria effectively ended the Peninsular War
examples: This war's decisive battle took place at Vitoria, and it saw British forces defend the Lines of Torres Vedras.

clues.clue: Installed his brother Joseph Bonaparte as King of Spain, governing under the 1808 Bayonne Statute, until Ferdinand VII was restored at the war's end
examples: Joseph I was made king during this conflict, but lost power when Ferdinand VII was restored.
Name this general who was made king during this conflict.

works.name: The Invasion of Russia (1812)
works.description: After Russia withdrew from the Continental System, Napoleon led over 600,000 soldiers of the Grande Armee into Russia in 1812, winning a costly pyrrhic victory over Mikhail Kutuzov at the Battle of Borodino before occupying a scorched Moscow; the catastrophic retreat, worsened by crossing the Berezina River and by Kutuzov's scorched-earth tactics, left only around 27,000 survivors and was famously visualized in Charles Minard's flow-map diagram.

clues.clue: Over 600,000 soldiers of the Grande Armee began the 1812 invasion of Russia, but only around 27,000 returned, with many dying of cold during the retreat
examples: Over 600,000 soldiers started this campaign but only 27,000 returned, many dying of cold during the retreat.

clues.clue: Won a pyrrhic victory over Mikhail Kutuzov at the Battle of Borodino, where attacks on the Bagration fleches and the Raevsky Redoubt caused heavy losses, before occupying a scorched Moscow
examples: the Battle of Borodino was part of what military campaign in which a country led by Alexander I was invaded by Napoleon?
The French Army occupied a purposefully scorched capital city during this campaign after their victory at the Battle of Borodino.

clues.clue: Struggled to supply the Grande Armee, encouraging soldiers to forage ("maraude") for food -- a tactic hindered by Kutuzov's scorched-earth strategy -- and is commonly credited with the quote "an army marches on its stomach"
examples: This ruler is commonly attributed the quote "an army marches on its stomach" and encouraged soldiers to "maraude" to acquire food.

clues.clue: The catastrophic retreat, in which the crossing of the Berezina River became a byword for disaster, was visualized in Charles Minard's pioneering flow-map diagram showing the army's rapidly thinning numbers
examples: A rapidly thinning snaking black line highlights the devastation of this campaign in a pioneering diagram by Charles Minard.
"Berezina" became a synonym for catastrophe among the instigators of this military campaign.

clues.clue: After being checked at Maloyaroslavets during the retreat, abandoned his troops to rush back to his capital and suppress the Malet conspiracy
examples: After being checked at Maloyaroslavets, he abandoned his troops by hurrying to his capital to suppress the Malet conspiracy.

works.name: Downfall: Abdication, Elba, Waterloo & Exile
works.description: Weakened by the Russian catastrophe, Napoleon fought the War of the Sixth Coalition and won the Six Days Campaign (including a victory at Vauchamps) but could not stop the Senat Conservateur from removing him from power; after abdicating and being exiled to Elba, he briefly returned during the Hundred Days before his final defeat at the Battle of Waterloo led to exile on the remote South Atlantic island of Saint Helena, where he died in 1821, and whose remains were exhumed nearly two decades later for a state funeral at Les Invalides.

clues.clue: Won the Six Days Campaign, including a victory at Vauchamps, during the War of the Sixth Coalition, but failed to stop the Senat Conservateur from removing him from power
examples: He won a victory at Vauchamps and three other battles in rapid succession in the Six Days Campaign but failed to stop the Senat Conservateur from removing him.

clues.clue: After his final defeat, was exiled to the remote South Atlantic island of Saint Helena (administered via the volcanic island of Ascension), where his residence, Longwood House, is now a museum
examples: Along with Tristan de Cunha, Ascension is administered from this other volcanic island located 800 miles to the southeast. Its capital is Jamestown, and its Longwood House has been converted into a museum.

clues.clue: Died on Saint Helena in 1821; nearly two decades later, his remains were exhumed and transported to Paris for a state funeral (the "Retour des Cendres") that concluded at Les Invalides in December 1840
examples: name this event that required exhuming a grave on Saint Helena and transporting the contents to Paris.
This event concluded on December 15, 1840 with a ceremony at Les Invalides.

works.name: Assassination Attempts & Conspiracies
works.description: Napoleon survived several attempts on his life, including a pastor's son who tried to stab him in Vienna in 1809 and an 1800 bombing plot on the Rue Saint-Nicaise using an "infernal machine."

clues.clue: A pastor's son, Friedrich Staps, attempted to assassinate him in Vienna in 1809; Napoleon offered to pardon Staps, who refused, declaring "To kill you would not have been a crime, but a duty!"
examples: A pastor's son named Friedrich Staps attempted to assassinate Napoleon while he was in this city in 1809.

clues.clue: Survived a bombing attempt with an "infernal machine" on the Rue Saint-Nicaise
examples: A different "infernal machine" was used in an attempted assassination of this ruler on the Rue Saint-Nicaise.

works.name: Depictions in Visual Art
works.description: Napoleon is among the most painted and sculpted figures in Western art, from Jacques-Louis David's Coronation and equestrian Crossing the Alps to Antoine-Jean Gros's Plague at Jaffa and Antonio Canova's nude Mars the Peacemaker, as well as later reinterpretations by artists from Ingres to Kehinde Wiley and Rene Magritte.

clues.clue: David's The Coronation of Napoleon (Le Sacre) depicts him crowning his wife Josephine at Notre Dame while Pope Pius VII, who probably did little, is shown bestowing a blessing; the artist inaccurately painted in Napoleon's absent mother, Madame Mere
examples: name this Jacques-Louis David painting, in which the title French Emperor prepares to place a crown on Josephine.
Although she was not at the event, this figure's mother was painted in.

clues.clue: Napoleon reportedly exclaimed "This is not a painting; one walks in this picture" upon first seeing The Coronation of Napoleon
examples: When this painting's title figure first saw it, he exclaimed: "This is not a painting; one walks in this picture."

clues.clue: David's equestrian portrait Napoleon Crossing the Alps shows him on a rearing horse (modeled on his horse Marengo) pointing forward, with the names "Hannibal" and "Karolus Magnus" inscribed on rocks; Napoleon refused to sit for it, so David had his young son pose on a ladder instead
examples: name this iconic equestrian portrait of the title French emperor about to cross a mountain range by Jacques-Louis David.
The subject of this work refused to sit for it, forcing the artist to have his son stand on a ladder and put the subject's uniform on.

clues.clue: Paul Delaroche later painted a more historically accurate, subdued version of the same scene, showing Napoleon riding a mule instead of a rearing horse
examples: Paul Delaroche was commissioned to create a more historically accurate version of this work.
Name this event depicted by David, as well as in a much more subdued version by Delaroche, whose subject sits on a mule in a brown overcoat instead of on a rearing horse.

clues.clue: Kehinde Wiley's 2005 parody of the Crossing the Alps composition replaces Napoleon with a modern Black man wearing camouflage fatigues and Timberland boots
examples: This event is set in front of a wallpaper-like red and gold backdrop... in a painting in which a man wears Timberland boots, camouflage fatigues, and a bandanna.
A work by Wiley parodies Jacque-Louis David's painting of this person "Crossing the Alps."

clues.clue: David's The Emperor Napoleon in His Study at the Tuileries popularized the "hand-in-waistcoat" pose, showing him having worked through the night on the Napoleonic Code, indicated by a clock reading 4:13 A.M. and a nearly burnt-out candle
examples: The National Gallery of Art holds a painting of this man in which a candle has nearly burnt through its wick, and a grandfather clock in the background reads 4:13 A.M.
This man appears disheveled after working into the early morning in a painting that popularized the "hand-in-waistcoat" pose for portrait subjects.

clues.clue: Antoine-Jean Gros's Napoleon Visiting the Plague Victims at Jaffa shows him touching the open sore of a plague victim at a makeshift hospital, inspired by David's Oath of the Horatii
examples: name this painting depicting a French military commander's bravery and compassion, a work by Jean Antoine Gros.
gamely touching the open sore of one of his soldiers at a makeshift hospital

clues.clue: Gros also painted Napoleon at the Battle of the Pyramids and at the aftermath of the Battle of Eylau, visiting soldiers dying in the snow
examples: Identify this Antoine-Jean Gros painting in which a First Consul places his finger on an infected soldier in the Middle East.
Bonaparte Visiting the Plague Victims of Jaffa was by Antoine-Jean Gros, who also painted Napoleon visiting soldiers dying in the snow on this battlefield.

clues.clue: Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres painted him in red as First Consul in Bonaparte, First Consul, and later in ornate ermine coronation robes in Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne
examples: This artist of The Turkish Bath and The Source depicted Napoleon in red in Bonaparte, First Consul before showing him in ornate ermine coronation robes in Napoleon I on his Imperial Throne.

clues.clue: Antonio Canova sculpted him nude as the Roman god of war in Napoleon as Mars the Peacemaker, holding a gilded statue of the goddess Victory, now displayed in a stairwell of London's Apsley House
examples: Name this historical figure who is portrayed as the Roman god of war in a large nude sculpture currently displayed in a stairwell designed by Robert Adam in London's Apsley House.
Name this Italian neoclassicist sculptor who also portrayed a reclining Paulina Bonaparte as Venus Victrix.

clues.clue: James Gillray's political cartoon The Plumb-pudding in Danger depicts him carving up a globe alongside British Prime Minister William Pitt
examples: This man appears on the right-hand side of James Gillray's political cartoon The Plumb-pudding in danger, in which he and another world leader carve up a globe.

clues.clue: Rene Magritte painted blue sky and clouds over a plaster reproduction of his death mask in The Future of Statues
examples: Magritte painted over a plaster reproduction of this man's death mask with blue sky and white clouds in his The Future of Statues.

clues.clue: Jean-Michel Basquiat depicted him as a wide-eyed duck alongside a blue star-ribbon in a work titled Napoleon's Stereotype as Portrayed Circa 1940
examples: A blue star-ribbon appears next to a caricature of this person as a wide-eyed duck in a Jean-Michel Basquiat work titled after [this man]'s Stereotype as Portrayed Circa 1940.

works.name: Depictions in Literature & Music
works.description: Napoleon's 1812 invasion of Russia forms the backdrop of Leo Tolstoy's War and Peace, and he looms over works from George Orwell's Animal Farm to Thackeray's Vanity Fair, Stendhal's The Charterhouse of Parma, and Beethoven's symphonic and piano works.

clues.clue: The backdrop of Leo Tolstoy's War and Peace, in which Pierre Bezukhov considers him "the Antichrist," calculates his name's numerology to 666 using Freemason methods, and feverishly plots to assassinate him
examples: A Freemason discovers this character's name sums to 666 in Hebrew numerical values.
This character is decried as "the Antichrist" at Anna Pavlovna's party by his would-be assassin, Pierre Bezukhov.

clues.clue: The antagonist pig dictator of George Orwell's Animal Farm, based on Stalin, is named for him
examples: Although he is based on Stalin, the antagonist of Animal Farm is named for this historical figure.

clues.clue: The Napoleonic Wars form the backdrop of William Makepeace Thackeray's Vanity Fair, in which he is defeated at Waterloo
examples: wars named for what man make up the backdrop to Vanity Fair, in which he is defeated at Waterloo?

clues.clue: An alternate-history war against him is chronicled in Susanna Clarke's novel Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell
examples: An alternate history of a conflict centered on this historical figure is chronicled in Jonathan Strange and Mr. Norrell.

clues.clue: Beethoven originally dedicated his Third Symphony ("Eroica") to him, but scratched out his name in fury upon learning he had crowned himself Emperor, rededicating it "to the memory of a great man"
examples: Beethoven originally dedicated his "Eroica" symphony to what French emperor?
That composer inscribed a piece "to the memory of a great man" after scratching out this man's name from its manuscript.

clues.clue: In Stendhal's The Charterhouse of Parma, protagonist Fabrizio del Dongo joins his Hundred Days campaign
examples: Fabrice del Dongo joins Napoleon's Hundred Days campaign in The Charterhouse of Parma, a novel by this author whose admiration for Napoleon is also evident.

clues.clue: Francois-Rene de Chateaubriand, an admirer, quipped of his writing that "destiny was mute, and Napoleon should have been"
examples: In reference to the Emperor's writing, this author quipped "destiny was mute, and Napoleon should have been."

clues.clue: Karl Marx contrasted his ascent to power with that of his nephew, writing that history repeats itself "the first time as tragedy, the second time as farce"
examples: Contrasting this man's ascent to power with that of his nephew led Karl Marx to write that history repeats itself "the first time as tragedy, the second time as farce."

clues.clue: Victor Hugo depicts the Battle of Waterloo and the death of Colonel Baron Pontmercy in the "Cosette" volume of Les Miserables
examples: This author and contemporary of Stendhal detailed the events of Waterloo and the death of Colonel Baron Pontmercy in the "Cosette" volume of his historical epic Les Miserables.

works.name: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte
works.description: Napoleon's brother Joseph Bonaparte ruled as puppet king of Naples and then Spain, his brother Jerome's marriage to American socialite Elizabeth Patterson was annulled by the family, and his sister Caroline's marriage to cavalry general Joachim Murat made him King of Naples; Napoleon also sold the Louisiana Territory to the United States in 1803, and his nephew Louis-Napoleon later seized power in France as Napoleon III.

clues.clue: In 1803, sold the Louisiana Territory to the United States for less than three cents an acre, nearly doubling the young nation's size
examples: In 1803, Napoleon Bonaparte sold off this territory for less than three cents an acre, nearly doubling America's size.

clues.clue: His brother Jerome Bonaparte's marriage to American socialite Elizabeth Patterson was disapproved of by the family; the American branch of the Bonapartes was based in Baltimore
examples: Name this family that disapproved of one member's marriage to socialite Elizabeth Patterson.

clues.clue: Made cavalry general Joachim Murat, husband of his sister Caroline Bonaparte, King of Naples
examples: Name this Napoleonic cavalry general who was Grand Duke of Berg until 1808, when he was made King of Naples by virtue of his marriage to Caroline Bonaparte.

clues.clue: His nephew, Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte, later seized power in France and founded the Second Empire as Napoleon III
examples: Name this nephew of Napoleon Bonaparte who established the Second Empire.

clues.clue: His name is attached to the "Napoleon complex," a term for compensatory behavior attributed to people of short stature
examples: This French emperor names a "complex" which attributes compensatory behaviors to people of short stature.

comprehensive_summary: Napoleon Bonaparte was born on Corsica while it was under French control and, as a young man, led riots against French troops there before joining the Republican army, where his command of the artillery at the 1793 Siege of Toulon launched his career. As a rising general under the Directory, he dissolved the leftist Pantheon Club (linked to Francois-Noel "Gracchus" Babeuf's Conspiracy of the Equals) before engineering the Coup of 18 Brumaire in 1799 -- with help from his brother, President of the Council of Five Hundred, who relocated the legislature to Saint-Cloud, and amid a bribe to Paul Barras -- to overthrow the Directory and become First Consul under the new Constitution of the Year VIII. His 1798 invasion of Egypt, meant to disrupt British interests in South Asia, saw him defeat the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids and capture Malta, but Horatio Nelson's victory at the Battle of the Nile stranded his army; checked at the Siege of Acre by Jazzar Pasha's resistance (aided by Haim Farhi and William Sidney Smith), he executed thousands of mostly Albanian prisoners at Jaffa and issued a proclamation to the Egyptian people defending his devotion to Islam, before the campaign ended in Jacques-Francois de Menou's Capitulation of Alexandria. As First Consul and Emperor, Napoleon promulgated the Napoleonic Code -- a unified civil code that purged the opposition Tribunate and which he predicted would be remembered longer than his "40 battles" -- restored the legal French nobility in 1804, reconciled with Pope Pius VII through the 1801 Concordat, and convened the Grand Sanhedrin under Rabbi David Sinzheim to formalize Jewish emancipation. He crowned himself Emperor at Notre Dame in December 1804, breaking with the tradition of Rheims and hastily marrying Josephine beforehand through his uncle Cardinal Joseph Fesch, an event immortalized in Jacques-Louis David's The Coronation of Napoleon. Militarily, he crushed the Third Coalition at the Battle of Austerlitz after the bloodless Ulm maneuver against General Karl Mack, shattered Prussia in the 1806 Jena campaign with generals like Louis Davout and Jozef Poniatowski, and defeated Russia to secure the 1807 Treaties of Tilsit with Tsar Alexander I -- signed on a raft on the Neman River -- establishing the Confederation of the Rhine and drawing Russia into his Continental System; he also pursued alliances with Qajar Iran (the unenacted Treaty of Finckenstein) and Oman. His Continental System, an embargo of Britain begun by the Berlin and Milan Decrees, provoked the British bombardment of Copenhagen and forced Portugal's royal family to flee to Brazil, and his 1807 invasion of Portugal opened the Peninsular War, where the Duke of Wellington's guerrilla-aided victories at Salamanca and Vitoria eventually expelled French forces (under his brother Joseph Bonaparte, installed as King of Spain) despite the defensive Lines of Torres Vedras. After Russia withdrew from the Continental System, Napoleon led over 600,000 soldiers into Russia in 1812, winning a pyrrhic victory over Mikhail Kutuzov at Borodino before occupying a scorched Moscow; the catastrophic retreat across the Berezina River, visualized in Charles Minard's flow-map diagram, left only around 27,000 survivors, and Napoleon abandoned his own troops near Maloyaroslavets to rush home and suppress the Malet conspiracy. He survived assassination attempts, including Friedrich Staps's 1809 knife attack in Vienna and an 1800 bombing on the Rue Saint-Nicaise. Weakened by the Russian disaster, he fought the War of the Sixth Coalition, winning the Six Days Campaign but failing to stop the Senat Conservateur from removing him; he abdicated, was exiled to Elba, briefly returned during the Hundred Days, and was finally defeated at the Battle of Waterloo, after which he was exiled to the remote island of Saint Helena, where he died in 1821 -- his remains exhumed nearly two decades later for a state funeral concluding at Les Invalides in 1840. In 1803 he had sold the Louisiana Territory to the United States for under three cents an acre; his brother Jerome's marriage to American Elizabeth Patterson was disapproved of by the family, his sister Caroline's marriage to cavalry general Joachim Murat made Murat King of Naples, and his nephew Louis-Napoleon later founded France's Second Empire as Napoleon III. Napoleon is among the most painted figures in Western art -- by David (the Coronation, the equestrian Crossing the Alps, later parodied by Kehinde Wiley, and The Emperor Napoleon in His Study at the Tuileries), by Antoine-Jean Gros (Plague Victims at Jaffa, the Pyramids, Eylau), by Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres, by Antonio Canova's nude Mars the Peacemaker sculpture, in James Gillray's satirical cartoon The Plumb-pudding in Danger, and in later reinterpretations by Rene Magritte and Jean-Michel Basquiat. He also looms over literature and music: as the historical backdrop of Tolstoy's War and Peace (where Pierre Bezukhov calls him "the Antichrist"), as the namesake of Orwell's Animal Farm dictator, in Thackeray's Vanity Fair and Stendhal's The Charterhouse of Parma, in Karl Marx's observation that history repeats "first as tragedy, then as farce," in Victor Hugo's Les Miserables, and in Beethoven's Third Symphony, whose dedication to Napoleon the composer angrily scratched out upon his self-coronation.
recursive_suggestions: Napoleon III — his nephew has his own dense answerline (Second Empire, Franco-Prussian War, Ems Dispatch, assassination attempts) that was almost entirely excluded from this page; strong candidate for a dedicated topic

Napoleon Chagnon — an unrelated 20th-century American anthropologist (Yanomamo fieldwork) who shares the first name "Napoleon" and repeatedly contaminates searches for this topic; entirely excluded here and worth flagging for future search hygiene
Peninsular War — has its own dense tossup answerline (4 questions) with content on Wellington, guerrilla warfare, and the Lines of Torres Vedras only partially used here; candidate for its own topic
Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres — recurring painter answerline with independent content (Grande Odalisque, Madame Moitessier, Turkish Bath) beyond his Napoleon portraits; distinct topic

The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Napoleon (Karl Marx) — has its own dense bonus/tossup answerline as a distinct political-theory text about Napoleon III, only lightly used here for the "tragedy/farce" quote
Tsar Alexander I of Russia — recurring figure (Tilsit, Congress of Vienna, 1812 invasion) with independent content; distinct topic
Antoine-Jean Gros — recurring painter answerline (Plague at Jaffa, Eylau, the Pyramids) with independent biographical content beyond these Napoleon works
House of Bonaparte — the wider family (Joseph, Jerome, Louis, Caroline, Murat, Napoleon III) has enough combined bonus presence to eventually support its own genealogical/legacy topic
